question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers:
This systematic review was registered with PROSPERO15 (registration
number CRD42016041425) and was conducted in
line with the PRISMA statement.16 Eligibility criteria for this
review included published case reports, case series and reviews
that (i) included patients with clinically diagnosed HS1 and
(ii) included identified sequence variants from patients with
HS or contained protein functional studies based on previously
identified sequence variants from patients with HS. No restrictions
on patient age, sex or ethnicity, or language restrictions
were applied.

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
This systematic review aims to identify all published reports
of genetic polymorphisms in both familial and sporadic HS. It
will also collate any protein or functional studies pertaining to
these identified variants. The identified sequence variants will
be critically evaluated using the ACMG standards and guidelines
for the interpretation of sequence variants12 using predefined
objective criteria.

Eligibility criteria for this
review included published case reports, case series and reviews
that (i) included patients with clinically diagnosed HS1 and
(ii) included identified sequence variants from patients with
HS or contained protein functional studies based on previously
identified sequence variants from patients with HS. No restrictions
on patient age, sex or ethnicity, or language restrictions
were applied

answers:

2016) and 'Epub ahead of print, and nonindexed citations', as
shown in Figure 1

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers:
Data collection was performed independently by two
authors, with any disagreements regarding inclusion of citations
being referred to a third author for mediation. Information
was collected using a standardized data collection form
(Appendix S2; see Supporting Information), with the principal
outcome of interest being identified sequence variants.

question: Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
answers:
Potential sources of bias in collating cases were acknowledged,
including publications bias and reporting bias regarding
the incidence of specific mutations and the rate of HS
kindred with no identified mutation.

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers:
Sixty-two
articles reported a total of 41 sequence variants, with 18
sequence variants having associated protein functional studies

Forty-one individual sequence variants were identified encompassing
the NCSTN, PSEN1, PSENEN and PSTPIP1 genes, coding for nicastrin,
presenilin 1, presenilin enhancer 2 and proline-serine-
threonine phosphatase-interacting protein 1, respectively.

The evaluation of each identified sequence variant against the
ACMG criteria12 is summarized in Table 1, with the criteria
explained fully in Table S1 (see Supporting Information).
Twenty-three variants were assessed as likely
pathogenic,11,17,18,20,23,28-36 17 were of uncertain significance11,19-
22,24-26,28,31,35 and one was benign.2

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers:
The identified sequence variants will
be critically evaluated using the ACMG standards and guidelines
for the interpretation of sequence variants12 using predefined
objective criteria.

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers:
Two authors independently
analysed each sequence variant using the collated data available
against the ACMG guidelines (Table 1). Any disagreement was
referred to a third independent author for mediation.

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
Two authors independently
analysed each sequence variant using the collated data available
against the ACMG guidelines (Table 1). Any disagreement was
referred to a third independent author for mediation.

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
answers:
Two authors independently
analysed each sequence variant using the collated data available
against the ACMG guidelines (Table 1). Any disagreement was
referred to a third independent author for mediation.

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
Potential sources of bias in collating cases were acknowledged,
including publications bias and reporting bias regarding
the incidence of specific mutations and the rate of HS
kindred with no identified mutation. Therefore no attempts
were made to quantify the most common sequence variants or
the proportion of kindreds without identified mutations due
to this bias being present

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers: nan

question: Was the relevance of identified studies to the review's research question appropriately considered?
answers:
This systematic review aims to identify all published reports
of genetic polymorphisms in both familial and sporadic HS. It
will also collate any protein or functional studies pertaining to
these identified variants.

Of the 41 discrete sequence variants, 18 had associated protein
or functional studies, including mRNA studies (eight variants),
10,23,26,30,32-35 transcribed protein studies (four
variants),10,20,26,30 functional studies of Notch activation (four
variants)11,17,22,38 and in silico predictive algorithms (two variants).
25,32,33 Twenty-seven variants were associated with
familial HS,9-11,17,19-23,25,27-30,32-35,37 six with sporadic
HS10,18,29,30,33 and one (presumed nonsignificant) with both
familial and sporadic cases of HS (NCSTN c.1180-5C>G).22,27
The inheritance of the remaining seven variants was not
reported.24,26,29,31

question: Did the reviewers avoid emphasizing results on the basis of their statistical significance?
answers: Yes

question: Did the review authors provide a list of excluded studies and justify the exclusions?
answers:
Of the 533 citations screened, 135 articles were identified for
full-text retrieval and review. Of these 135 full-text articles,
70 were excluded due to not pertaining to gene studies or
functional analysis, with three articles being duplicate reports
of included cases either in abstract or review form.
